entries.symbolic_meaning:
The Seven Liberal Arts and Sciences represent a comprehensive system of knowledge and intellectual development. Geometry, in particular, symbolizes the order, precision, and foundational principles necessary for creation and understanding the universe, reflecting the architect's ability to plan and execute designs.

Freemasonry primarily regards the internal, not the external, qualifications of a man. Geometry is established as the basis of the Masonic art, possessing a divine and moral nature. It is seen as a science that not only proves the wonderful properties of nature but also demonstrates the more important truths of morality.

The invocation of Deity symbolizes the spiritual foundation of all Masonic endeavors and the reliance on divine guidance. The Festival of St. John the Evangelist, while a social occasion, is consecrated to duty, representing the ongoing commitment to Masonic principles and the renewal of vows by those taking on leadership. The Marshal's address outlines the symbolic and practical influence of Freemasonry, emphasizing its role as a moral and educational force in society, promoting universal brotherhood and adherence to divine and moral law.

The induction of officers signifies the continuity of leadership, the structured operation of the Lodge, and the fulfillment of the Craft's purposes through chosen individuals.

The five steps allude to the five orders of architecture (Tuscan, Doric, Ionic, Corinthian, Composite) and the five human senses (hearing, seeing, feeling, smelling, tasting), emphasizing the first three for Masonic recognition. The seven steps allude to various significant sevens, but primarily the seven liberal arts and sciences (grammar, rhetoric, logic, arithmetic, geometry, music, astronomy), symbolizing the pursuit of knowledge. 'Shibboleth' denotes 'Plenty' and serves as a password, originating from the biblical story of Jephthah and the Ephraimites, signifying the ability to distinguish a true brother from an impostor. 'Jachin' is the word of a Fellow Craft, revealed through a specific process, representing a deeper level of recognition. The Corn of nourishment, Wine of refreshment, and Oil of joy are the symbolic wages, representing sustenance, comfort, and happiness derived from diligent labor and adherence to Masonic principles.

The jewels of a Fellow Craft (attentive ear, instructive tongue, faithful breast) symbolize peace, harmony, and strength, and the secure reception and preservation of Masonic mysteries. The letter 'G' primarily symbolizes Geometry, representing the basis of Masonry, the ability to trace nature's secrets, and to comprehend the power, wisdom, and goodness of the Grand Artificer of the Universe. It also signifies the understanding of celestial mechanics and the origins of human art and society. Its higher signification is the sacred name of Deity, demanding universal reverence.

Masonry primarily regards the internal, not the external, qualifications of a man. Geometry, considered synonymous with Masonry, is described as being of a divine and moral nature, providing useful knowledge by demonstrating both the wonderful properties of nature and the more important truths of morality, serving as the basis of the Masonic art.

Boaz denotes strength and Jachin denotes establishment; collectively, they allude to the Scripture: "In strength shall this house be established." Net-work symbolizes union due to its connection. Lily-work symbolizes peace due to its whiteness and purity. Pomegranates symbolize plenty due to the exuberance of their seed. The globes on the pillars denote the universality of Masonry, implying a Mason's charity should be equally extensive. The hollow nature of the pillars symbolizes their ability to withstand inundations or conflagrations and their role in containing the archives of Masonry. The three steps of the winding stairs allude to the three principal supports in Masonry (wisdom, strength, beauty), the three stages in human life (youth, manhood, age), and the three degrees in Masonry (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason). The five steps allude to the five orders in architecture (Tuscan, Doric, Ionic, Corinthian, Composite) and the five human senses (hearing, seeing, feeling, smelling, tasting). The seven steps allude to various septenary concepts, but more especially the seven liberal arts and sciences (grammar, rhetoric, logic, arithmetic, geometry, music, astronomy), signifying the high estimation of the number seven among Masons.

Secret societies like the Essenes conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures. The breast, as the abode of fortitude, teaches the candidate to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity with patience rather than betraying secrets. Hebrew words ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) are symbolically interpreted to mean fortifying oneself with higher sciences and preparing to receive moral light. Shibboleth signifies both an ear of grain and a stream of water, with its symbolic interpretation increasing interest as one advances through the 'field of corn by the river-side'. The payment of wages without fear or scruple symbolizes the employers' entitlement to unlimited confidence.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate is taught the importance of intellectual pursuit and the foundational nature of the Liberal Arts and Sciences, with a special emphasis on Geometry. This lesson encourages the candidate to understand how these disciplines contribute to order, structure, and a deeper comprehension of both the physical world and abstract concepts, serving as a basis for further Masonic learning and personal development.

The Fellow Craft candidate is taught that true worth lies in internal character and that increasing knowledge leads to improved social interaction. They are reminded of their duties to uphold Masonic laws and to interact with brethren with fairness, kindness, and justice. A key lesson is the importance of intellectual and moral self-improvement through the study of the liberal arts, with a particular focus on geometry as a foundation for both natural and moral understanding. The candidate is expected to maintain virtuous conduct, consistent with the principles of the Institution.

Candidates are taught the importance of invoking divine blessing in all undertakings. They learn that Freemasonry is a society founded on ancient traditions and profound principles, not merely social enjoyment. Key lessons include the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, the importance of loving God and one's neighbor, and the cultivation of virtues such as liberty, equality, fraternity, and adherence to law and order. The Craft encourages intellectual growth through liberal arts and sciences, promotes education, and instills a sense of duty towards God, fellow men, and country, aiming to make individuals better citizens.

The passage, addressed to 'good friends,' explains the fundamental purpose of the Masonic Craft and highlights the organized process of establishing leadership within the Lodge for a defined period, emphasizing order and continuity.

The journey through the Winding Stairs teaches the candidate the importance of intellectual development through the liberal arts and sciences. The challenges at the doors emphasize the necessity of proving one's identity and proficiency through correct words, grips, and tokens. The story of Shibboleth imparts lessons on fidelity, adherence to truth, and the consequences of imposture. The reception in the Middle Chamber signifies the candidate's advancement and entitlement to the moral and intellectual rewards (wages) earned through labor and study as a Fellow Craft.

Candidates are taught the importance of attentive listening, truthful communication, and the faithful preservation of Masonic knowledge. They learn that geometry is fundamental to understanding both the physical world and the divine order established by the Grand Artificer. Furthermore, they are instructed in the profound reverence due to the Deity, symbolized by the letter 'G', and the enduring nature of Masonic principles despite historical destruction.

The Fellow Craft is taught that true Masonic worth comes from internal character. They are expected to uphold the Order's laws, exercise fairness, compassion, and justice when dealing with the faults of others, and to pursue intellectual and moral improvement through the study of the liberal arts, particularly geometry, which is presented as a foundation for understanding both the natural world and moral principles. Continued virtuous conduct is also a key expectation.

Masons should strive for charity as extensive as the universality of Masonry. The three principal supports of Masonry (wisdom, strength, beauty) are fundamental to the craft. The three stages of human life (youth, manhood, age) remind Masons of their journey and development. The three degrees of Masonry represent a progressive path of knowledge. The five human senses, especially hearing, seeing, and feeling, are essential for Masonic recognition and communication, allowing Masons to know each other in both light and darkness. The seven liberal arts and sciences are crucial for intellectual development and understanding, emphasizing the importance of education within Masonry.

Candidates are taught to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity, distress, pain, or disappointment with patience. This fortitude prevents the bitter stings of remorse that result from betraying secrets entrusted under solemn obligation. The neophyte should prepare to receive moral light through higher sciences. Candidates are directed to an attentive study of the liberal arts and sciences. The legend of wages paid without fear or scruple teaches the importance of unlimited confidence in employers.
tradition_notes: Geometry is traditionally held in high reverence by Masons, considered the 'foundation of architecture and the root of mathematics'.
Freemasonry regards the internal, not the external, qualifications of a man.
The study of the liberal arts, particularly geometry, is earnestly recommended as a valuable branch of education.
Geometry is established as the basis of the Masonic art and is considered to be of a divine and moral nature.
Freemasonry has a tradition of invoking a blessing of Deity before any great or important undertaking.
The Festival of St. John the Evangelist is a traditional observance in Freemasonry, consecrated to duty and promoting social enjoyment and brotherly love.
Freemasonry's origins are described as ancient, with its beginnings not recorded on written pages of history.

The practice of inducting officers to preside over a Lodge for a specific term (e.g., twelve months) is a traditional aspect of Masonic governance and ensures the continuous operation and leadership of the Lodge.
The first three human senses (hearing, seeing, feeling) have ever been highly esteemed among Masons for recognition.
The number seven has ever been held in high estimation among Masons, especially due to the seven liberal arts and sciences.
The word 'Shibboleth' originated from a historical quarrel between Jephthah and the Ephraimites and was adopted by ancient brethren to distinguish a friend from a foe.
The letter 'G' is generally placed on the wall back of the Master's seat and above his head, or sometimes suspended in front of the Master by a cord or wire.
Freemasonry is noted to have survived the 'ruthless hand of ignorance' and 'devastations of war', including the destruction of the Temple of Solomon.
Geometry, or Masonry, originally synonymous terms.
The pillars Boaz and Jachin are named, and their individual and collective scriptural allusions are provided.
Hiram Abiff is identified as the Grand Master who cast the pillars and other holy vessels.
The specific location for casting the pillars is given as the banks of the river Jordan, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zaredatha.
The hollow nature of the pillars is explained as a means to withstand inundations or conflagrations and to contain the archives of Masonry.
The number seven has always been held in high estimation among Masons for various reasons, including its connection to the liberal arts and sciences.
Calmet asserts that St. John was an Essene, a secret society of his day that conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures, which may be termed Freemasonry under another name.
The Jewish Cabala includes a rule called Transposition, used to find meaning in anagrammatically formed words.
Brother Rosenberg, a Jewish Mason, used Transposition to interpret ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) in Hebrew, relating them to receiving higher sciences and moral light.
SHIBBOLETH in Hebrew has two significations: an ear of grain and a stream of water.
The historical account of the Ephraimites and Gileadites illustrates how the word 'Shibboleth' was used as a test, exploiting a peculiarity in the Ephraimites' speech organs.

The Fellow Crafts' Degree Lecture's first section recapitulates the ceremonies of passing a candidate, and the second section discusses the ancient division into operative and speculative Masons and the liberal arts and sciences.

According to Masonic legends, workmen's wages were paid in their respective Lodges, and later in the Middle Chamber, which was accessible by a winding staircase guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.

entries.title: Working Tools
entries.short_summary: The plumb, level, and square of the Fellow Craft.
entries.full_summary:
<!-- PDF_STAGE5:deeper-meaning-of-fc-degree:section-0005 -->
### Enrichment from The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree
Source section: Opening the Lodge in the Second Degree

The opening of the Lodge in the Second Degree signifies a profound transition from the concrete, body-dominant focus of the First Degree to a more abstract realm of mind and soul. This progression, termed 'going up,' requires a corresponding mental uplift from all present. The ritual emphasizes this shift through specific elements like the position of the square and compasses, and the distinctive knocks. The Square, with its horizontal and vertical arms, serves as a metaphor for the ideal relationship between passive bodily energies and active, aspiring mental and soulful faculties. All Masons present must demonstrate this right relationship through a ceremonial gesture for the Lodge to be truly 'opened on the Square.' The unique pattern of two joined knocks in the Fellowcraft Degree further symbolizes the union of soul and body, indicating a stage where mind and soul dominate the body, though pure spiritual faculties are not yet in full control.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:deeper-meaning-of-fc-degree:section-0005 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0031 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Jewels of a Lodge

This section defines the six Jewels of a Lodge, categorized as three movable and three immovable. The immovable jewels are the Square, Level, and Plumb, symbolizing morality, equality, and rectitude of conduct, respectively. The movable jewels are the Rough Ashlar, Perfect Ashlar, and Trestle-Board. The Rough Ashlar represents humanity's rude and imperfect natural state, while the Perfect Ashlar signifies the state of perfection achievable through virtuous education, personal effort, and divine blessing. The Trestle-Board serves as a reminder for Masons to align their spiritual building with the divine designs laid down by the Supreme Architect of the Universe.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0031 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0049 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Working Tools

This section defines the working tools of a Fellow Craft: the Plumb, Square, and Level. It first describes their practical uses for operative masons in construction, then elaborates on their more profound symbolic meanings for Free and Accepted Masons, emphasizing moral conduct, virtue, and the contemplation of mortality.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0049 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0053 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Working Tools

This section introduces the working tools of a Fellow Craft: the Plumb, Square, and Level. It contrasts their practical use by operative masons for construction tasks like raising perpendiculars, squaring work, and laying horizontals, with their symbolic application for Free and Accepted Masons. Symbolically, the Plumb teaches upright conduct, the Square guides virtuous actions, and the Level reminds us of life's journey towards death.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0053 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0008 -->
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Preparation for the Obligation: Dress and the Square

This section details the preparation of the candidate for the obligation in the Second Degree, focusing on the specific attire and the symbolic use of the Square. It explains the historical context of the candidate's dress, noting that ancient Masonic practice for this degree resembled the current Third Degree attire, and connects it to the practical dress of working masons. The importance of the Square is highlighted through its physical placement with the candidate and its representation in the degree's signs.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0008 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0016 -->
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: The Working Tools and Their Moral Application

This section introduces the working tools of a stonemason, which are also found on the seats of the Worshipful Master and Wardens. As Free & Accepted Masons, these tools are applied to morals, with a longer explanation teaching important lessons, including duty to God. The presentation concludes with the aspiration to ascend to 'immortal mansions whence all goodness emanates,' emphasizing God's presence and the requirement of 'Holiness to the Lord,' as declared by the seal of the United Grand Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0016 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0039 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft Word and Grips

This section details the ritualistic examination of a Fellow Craft candidate, covering the exchange of the pass grip, real grip, and the word 'Jachin' with the Worshipful Master. It then describes the candidate's examination by the Junior and Senior Wardens, involving the duegard and sign of a Fellow Craft, and the pass word 'Shibboleth'. The ritual also includes specific instructions on how a Fellow Craft Mason should wear their apron, with an allusion to King Solomon's Temple, and concludes with the presentation of the working tools: the plumb, square, and level.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0039 -->

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The term 'going up' to the Second Degree symbolizes an uplift of mind to a higher, more abstract level, moving beyond concrete things of time and space to the realm of mind and soul. The Square's two arms, horizontal and vertical, metaphorically represent the right relationship between passive bodily energies (horizontal) and active, aspiring higher faculties of mind and soul (vertical). The joined knocks in the Fellowcraft Degree indicate that the soul and body are now working in union, signifying the dominance of mind/soul over the body, though purely spiritual faculties have not yet gained control.

The Square symbolizes morality. The Level symbolizes equality. The Plumb symbolizes rectitude of conduct. The Rough Ashlar symbolizes our rude and imperfect state by nature. The Perfect Ashlar symbolizes the state of perfection we hope to achieve through virtuous education, our own endeavors, and the blessing of God. The Trestle-Board symbolizes the rules and designs laid down by the Supreme Architect of the Universe in the great books of nature and revelation, which serve as a guide for both operative and speculative Masons to erect their spiritual building.

For Free and Accepted Masons, the Plumb symbolizes walking uprightly before God and man. The Square represents squaring one's actions by the square of virtue. The Level signifies that all Masons are traveling upon the level of time towards the afterlife, 'that undiscovered country from whose bourne no traveler returns'.

For Free and Accepted Masons, the Plumb admonishes us to walk uprightly in our stations before God and man. The Square teaches us to square our actions by the square of virtue. The Level reminds us that we are traveling upon the level of time to that undiscovered country from whose bourne no traveler returns, symbolizing the journey towards death.

The specific attire and the prominent placement of the Square during the obligation emphasize the practical and moral principles associated with the Fellow Craft degree. The Square, as a working tool, symbolizes rectitude, morality, and the proper conduct of life, reinforced by its presence in the degree's signs and the candidate's posture.

The working tools, typically used by stonemasons, are symbolically applied to morals by Free & Accepted Masons. The aspiration to 'ascend to those immortal mansions whence all goodness emanates' symbolizes a spiritual goal and connection to God. The phrase 'Holiness to the Lord' from the United Grand Lodge seal signifies the ultimate moral and spiritual standard required.

The duegard of a Fellow Craft alludes to the manner in which the candidate's hands were placed when taking the solemn oath of a Fellow Craft. The sign of a Fellow Craft alludes to the penalty of the obligation.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate, and all Masons present, are called to achieve a corresponding uplift of mind, transitioning from a focus on concrete matters to the more abstract world of mind and soul. They must learn to allow bodily energies to be passive while their higher faculties of mind and soul become active and aspire upwards, demonstrating this proper relationship through a ceremonial gesture. The lesson emphasizes the union and dominance of mind and soul over the body.

Candidates are taught the importance of moral conduct, equality, and rectitude in their lives. They learn that they begin in an imperfect state, like the Rough Ashlar, but can strive for perfection through education, personal effort, and divine guidance, becoming like the Perfect Ashlar. The Trestle-Board instructs them to build their spiritual lives according to divine principles and designs.

Candidates are taught to apply the principles of the working tools to their moral and spiritual lives: to maintain integrity and uprightness (Plumb), to act virtuously (Square), and to reflect on the transient nature of life and the inevitability of death (Level).

Candidates are taught to apply the working tools to their moral and spiritual lives: to conduct themselves with integrity and uprightness, to ensure their actions are virtuous, and to contemplate the transient nature of life and the inevitability of death.

The preparation, including the specific dress and interaction with the Square, teaches the candidate about the historical roots of Masonic practice and the enduring importance of the Square as a symbol of moral conduct and the duties expected in the Fellow Craft degree.

Candidates are taught that the working tools of a stonemason have a moral application in Freemasonry, guiding them towards self-improvement and a deeper understanding of their duty to God. The lesson emphasizes the pursuit of holiness and goodness, with the ultimate goal of spiritual ascent.

The candidate learns the specific grips, words, and signs of the Fellow Craft degree, including the pass grip, real grip, the word 'Jachin', the duegard, the sign, and the pass word 'Shibboleth'. They are instructed on the proper way to wear the Fellow Craft apron, with the flap down and a corner turned up, symbolizing the practice during the building of King Solomon's Temple. The candidate is also introduced to the working tools of a Fellow Craft: the plumb, square, and level.
tradition_notes: The traditional phrase 'going up' to the Second Degree is used to describe the progression.
The specific pattern of knocks for the Fellowcraft Degree, with two joined, is a traditional element.
The declaration that the Lodge is 'opened on the Square' is a traditional part of the ceremony.
The Jewels of a Lodge are traditionally classified into six: three movable and three immovable.
The Square, Level, and Plumb are identified as immovable jewels.
The Rough Ashlar, Perfect Ashlar, and Trestle-Board are identified as movable jewels.
Originally, there was only one grade of Mason in a Lodge: Fellow or Master of the Craft.
In ancient Masonry, the candidate was attired as they are now in the Third Degree.
Working masons historically rolled up their sleeves and wore pants that only came to the knee.
The practice of applying working tools to morals is a distinctive aspect of Free & Accepted Masonry, differentiating it from operative masonry.
The phrase 'Holiness to the Lord' is a declaration found on the seal of the United Grand Lodge.
There are two ways to give the word 'Jachin': by halving it ('Ja', 'Chin') or giving it whole, with the halving method considered more correct.
At the building of King Solomon's Temple, Fellow Crafts wore their aprons with the flap turned down and a corner turned up, a tradition continued in the degree.

entries.full_summary:
<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0054 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

This section introduces the Fellow Craft candidate to the concepts of Operative and Speculative Masonry, defining each and emphasizing the moral and religious aspects of Speculative Masonry, including the contemplation of creation and homage to Deity. It also details the historical practice of ancient brethren working in both forms and consecrating the seventh day for rest and contemplation. The section concludes with a biblical description of the two pillars of brass, their dimensions, and features like chapiters, network, and pomegranates, as they would appear on the passage to the Middle Chamber.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0054 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0074 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Section

This section describes the Third Section of a degree, focusing on hieroglyphical emblems, moral lessons, and extensive details regarding the construction of King Solomon's Temple. It covers the temple's foundation, location on Mount Moriah, construction duration, the absence of tool sounds during building, and the vast workforce involved, including Grand Masters, Masters, Fellow Crafts, and Entered Apprentices. The section also elaborates on the two celebrated pillars placed at the temple's entrance, explaining their symbolic significance as a memorial to the Israelites' deliverance from Egyptian bondage, referencing the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0074 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0011 -->
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Halving the Word and the Position of the Pillars

This section clarifies two aspects of Masonic ritual: the historical practice of 'halving the word' and the correct orientation of the two pillars. It notes that the method of halving the word can vary between jurisdictions, potentially causing confusion. Regarding the pillars, it explains that their description as 'looking East' differs in perspective between Solomon's Temple (looking out) and a Masonic lodge (looking in). It also highlights that the commemoration of an Assistant High Priest by the second pillar is found in the Pseudepigrapha, not the Volume of the Sacred Law.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0011 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0012 -->
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: The Two Pillars Conjoined

This section explains that the two pillars, traditionally associated with the Fellow Craft degree, were originally part of a single ceremony, as evidenced by early floor-cloths and the historical practice of conferring both Apprentice and Fellow degrees on the same evening, particularly at the Grand Lodge of All England at York until 1780. The ritualistic question posed by the Senior Warden further supports this conjoint meaning, which is linked to an embellished translation of 1 Kings, chapter 8, verse 13.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0012 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0041 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft's Apron

This section details the preparation and initial steps of the Fellow Craft degree, focusing on the candidate's reception. It describes the conductor leading the candidate before the altar, the exchange of the duegard and sign, and the subsequent arrangement of the Lodge for the second reception. Key elements include the placement of two large pillars and fifteen pieces of painted board representing three, five, and seven steps, leading to the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple. The candidate is then dressed with a Fellow Craft's apron, and the conductor explains the ascent through the winding stairs to receive instructions on wages and jewels. The section concludes with an introduction to Operative and Speculative Masonry, defining their respective applications and moral lessons.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0041 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0042 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: The Pillars Boaz and Jachin

This section describes the introduction of the Fellow Craft candidate to the two brazen pillars, Boaz and Jachin, located at the outer porch of King Solomon's Temple. It details their names, symbolic meanings (strength and establishment), dimensions, and adornments, which include network, lily-work, and pomegranates, symbolizing Unity, Peace, and Plenty. Each pillar is topped with a globe, representing the earth and heavens, signifying the universality of Masonry and encouraging the study of sciences like astronomy and geography. The text also distinguishes between Operative and Speculative Masonry, emphasizing the moral and religious aspects of the latter, and notes the ancient brethren's practice of resting on the seventh day.

This section describes the casting of hollow pillars at Succoth and Zaredatha, noting their design to withstand disasters and their traditional role in containing Masonic archives. It then introduces the winding stairs leading to the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple, which consist of three, five, and seven steps. The first three steps are symbolically linked to the three principal stages of human life (youth, manhood, and old age), corresponding to the Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason degrees, outlining their respective duties and aspirations. These steps also allude to the three principal supports of Masonry (Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty) and the three principal officers of the Lodge (Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden).
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0042 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0047 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft Lecture: Middle Chamber Work

This section details the Fellow Craft's journey to the Middle Chamber, beginning with the encounter of the two pillars, Boaz and Jachin, at the entrance of King Solomon's Temple. It describes their names, symbolic meanings (strength and establishment), dimensions, composition (molten brass), and adornments, including chapiters, net-work, lily-work, pomegranates (denoting unity, peace, and plenty), and globes (representing the universality of Masonry). The narrative then proceeds to the winding stairs, explaining the symbolism of its three, five, and seven steps, which allude to Masonic supports, human life stages, degrees in Masonry, orders of architecture, human senses (emphasizing hearing, seeing, feeling for recognition), and the seven liberal arts and sciences, respectively. The section concludes with the candidate reaching the outer door of the Middle Chamber, tyled by the Junior Warden, and the method of gaining admission.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0047 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0070 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Three Grand Pillars and Monitor Emblems

This section details the three grand Masonic pillars: Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty, represented by King Solomon, Hiram King of Tyre, and Hiram Abiff, respectively. It describes the construction of King Solomon's Temple, including the number of columns, pilasters, and workmen (Grand Masters, Masters/Overseers, Fellow Crafts, and Entered Apprentices). The text also outlines the constitution of lodges for each degree (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason) and their traditional meeting places within King Solomon's Temple. Finally, it introduces monitorial emblems found on the Master's carpet, specifically the Three Steps (symbolizing youth, manhood, and age across the degrees) and the Pot of Incense (emblematic of a pure heart and gratitude). The Master's carpet of emblems is noted as a part of the Third Degree initiation.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0070 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0080 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Third Section.

This section, presented in a Q&A format, concludes the Master Mason's Degree by reviewing key aspects of King Solomon's Temple, including its construction duration, structural supports (columns and pillars of Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty), and the workforce involved. It details the constitution and usual meeting places for Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, and Master Mason lodges. The section then enumerates two classes of emblems specific to the Master Mason Degree, such as the Pot of Incense, Beehive, All-seeing Eye, Anchor and Ark, Forty-seventh Problem of Euclid, Hour-Glass, Scythe, Three Steps, Setting-Maul, Spade, Coffin, Grave, and Sprig of Acacia. Finally, it addresses the lost word of a Master Mason, its substitute 'Mah-Hah-Bone' given on the five points of fellowship, and notes that the original word was later found and used in the Royal Arch Degree.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0080 -->

Ancient brethren wrought six days and rested on the seventh.
The temple was founded in the fourth year of Solomon's reign, on the second day of the month Zif.
It was located on Mount Moriah, near where Abraham was to offer Isaac and where David appeased the destroying angel.
Construction took over seven years, during which it reportedly did not rain in the daytime.
No sound of axe, hammer, or iron tool was heard during construction.
The temple was supported by 1453 pillars and 2906 pilasters, all of Parian marble.
The workforce included 3 Grand Masters, 3300 Masters/Overseers, 80000 Fellow Crafts, and 70000 Entered Apprentices.
Two celebrated pillars were placed in front of the temple's porch, one on the left and one on the right.
The lettering or halving of a 'secret' word was a common practice in the 18th Century or before.
In some Masonic rituals, especially in the USA, the letters for the word are given haphazardly, unlike the order used in England.

Candidate is dressed with a white apron, flap turned down, as worn by Fellow Crafts.
Conductor opens the Lodge-door and leads the candidate by the left arm through the door in front of the pillars.
Conductor explains the ascent through a porch by a flight of winding stairs to the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple.
Conductor leads the candidate through the Lodge-door in front of the pillars.

Stepping to the three steps on the floor or carpet

Pillars' initial dimensions: 35 cubits height, 12 cubits circumference, 4 cubits diameter.
Chapiters on pillars were 5 cubits in height.
Pillars adorned with wreaths of net-work, lily-work, and pomegranates.
Pillars adorned with two large globes or balls.
Entire height of pillars was 40 cubits.
Pillars contained maps and charts of celestial and terrestrial bodies.
Pillars composed of molten or cast brass.
Pillars cast by Hiram Abiff on the banks of the river Jordan, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zaredatha.
Pillars cast hollow with a thickness of four inches or a hand's breadth.
Ascending a long, winding staircase or flight of winding stairs, consisting of three, five, and seven steps.
Reaching the outer door of the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple.

Gaining admission by the pass and token of the pass of a Fellow Craft.
The Master calls the candidate's attention to the three grand Masonic pillars.

King Solomon's Temple was built in seven years, during which it did not rain in the daytime to avoid obstructing workmen.
The Temple was supported by 1453 columns and 2906 pilasters, all of Parian marble.
An Entered Apprentices' Lodge is constituted by seven members: one Master and six Entered Apprentices, meeting on the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple.
A Fellow Crafts' Lodge is constituted by five members: two Masters and three Fellow Crafts, meeting in the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple.
A Masters' Lodge is constituted by three Master Masons, meeting in the Sanctum Sanctorum, or Holy of Holies, of King Solomon's Temple.
The substitute word 'Mah-Hah-Bone' is given on the five points of fellowship and in low breath.
The missing word was found after 470 years and is used in the Royal Arch Degree.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
Operative Masonry refers to the proper application of architectural rules for structure, figure, strength, beauty, proportion, and correspondence. Speculative Masonry teaches to subdue passions, act upon the square, maintain a good report, secrecy, and charity. It is interwoven with religion, obliging Masons to pay rational homage to Deity, contemplate the glorious works of creation, and adore the divine Creator. The seventh day of rest is consecrated for contemplation of creation and adoration of the Creator.

The section illustrates hieroglyphical emblems and inculcates moral lessons. The two celebrated pillars at the temple entrance symbolize the happy deliverance of the Israelites from Egyptian bondage and commemorate the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud, which provided light and darkness respectively during the Exodus. Their placement ensured the event was continually before the eyes of those going to and from divine worship.

The distinction in pillar orientation between Solomon's Temple and the lodge (looking out vs. looking in) symbolically represents different perspectives or focuses within the Masonic context. The 'halving the word' practice is a ritualistic element, inherently symbolic of esoteric knowledge and its transmission.

The conjoint meaning of the two pillars is an embellished translation of 1 Kings, chapter 8, verse 13, indicating their intended unity and significance within the ritual.

Operative Masonry symbolizes the practical application of architectural rules to create figure, strength, and beauty in structures, providing shelter and demonstrating human wisdom, science, and industry. Speculative Masonry symbolizes the moral and ethical lessons derived from these principles, teaching candidates to subdue passions, act upon the square, maintain a good report, keep secrecy, and practice charity. The ascent through winding stairs to the Middle Chamber represents a journey of learning and progression to receive further instruction and understanding.

The pillars Boaz and Jachin collectively denote establishment and strength, alluding to the Scripture passage: "In strength shall this house be established." The network ornamentation on the chapiters symbolizes unity due to its connection. The lily-work signifies purity and peace, derived from its whiteness and retired growth. The pomegranates represent plenty, owing to their abundant seeds. The globes atop the chapiters symbolize the universality of Masonry and that a Mason's charity should be equally extensive, also serving to illustrate astronomical and geographical phenomena.

The hollow pillars symbolize resilience against inundation and conflagration, and are traditionally said to have contained the archives of Masonry. The three steps of the winding stairs symbolize the three principal stages of human life: youth, manhood, and old age, each corresponding to a Masonic degree. They also represent the three principal supports of Masonry: Wisdom to contrive, Strength to support, and Beauty to adorn all great undertakings. Furthermore, they allude to the three principal officers of the Lodge: the Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden.

Boaz denotes strength and Jachin denotes establishment; collectively, they allude to the Scripture: "In strength shall this house be established." Net-work symbolizes union due to its connection. Lily-work symbolizes peace due to its whiteness and purity. Pomegranates symbolize plenty due to the exuberance of their seed. The globes on the pillars denote the universality of Masonry, implying a Mason's charity should be equally extensive. The hollow nature of the pillars symbolizes their ability to withstand inundations or conflagrations and their role in containing the archives of Masonry. The three steps of the winding stairs allude to the three principal supports in Masonry (wisdom, strength, beauty), the three stages in human life (youth, manhood, age), and the three degrees in Masonry (Entered Apprentice, Fellow Craft, Master Mason). The five steps allude to the five orders in architecture (Tuscan, Doric, Ionic, Corinthian, Composite) and the five human senses (hearing, seeing, feeling, smelling, tasting). The seven steps allude to various septenary concepts, but more especially the seven liberal arts and sciences (grammar, rhetoric, logic, arithmetic, geometry, music, astronomy), signifying the high estimation of the number seven among Masons.

The Three Grand Pillars represent Wisdom (King Solomon), Strength (Hiram, King of Tyre), and Beauty (Hiram Abiff). The Three Steps are emblematic of the three principal stages of human life: youth, manhood, and age, corresponding to Entered Apprentices, Fellow Crafts, and Master Masons. The Pot of Incense symbolizes a pure heart, an acceptable sacrifice to the Deity, and the need for hearts to glow with gratitude.

The three grand columns or pillars supporting the Temple are named Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty. Wisdom represents King Solomon, who conceived the Temple; Strength represents Hiram, King of Tyre, who supported the undertaking; and Beauty represents Hiram Abiff, whose craftsmanship adorned the Temple. The emblems of the Master Mason Degree, divided into two classes, serve as symbolic lessons, though their specific explanations are noted as monitorial. The Sprig of Acacia is a significant emblem. The lost word and its substitute, Mah-Hah-Bone, symbolize a profound truth or secret that was lost and later replaced, with the original word eventually being recovered in a higher degree.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate learns the distinction between Operative and Speculative Masonry, understanding that modern Freemasonry is primarily Speculative, focusing on moral and spiritual development. They are taught the importance of self-governance, ethical conduct (acting upon the square, good report, secrecy, charity), and a deep reverence for the Creator and His creation, including the practice of setting aside time for rest and contemplation.

The section emphasizes the importance of peace and good fellowship among workmen, as exemplified by King Solomon's wise organization of the temple builders, which prevented envy, discord, or confusion. It also highlights the significance of divine guidance and deliverance, as symbolized by the temple's pillars, reminding candidates of historical and spiritual liberation.

Candidates should be aware that ritualistic elements, such as the order of letters in a word, can vary between jurisdictions, which might be puzzling. Understanding the correct orientation of the pillars, distinguishing between their placement in Solomon's Temple and a lodge, is crucial for comprehending lodge symbolism.

The candidate learns about the progression within the Fellow Craft degree, the significance of the Middle Chamber as a place of instruction, and the fundamental distinction between Operative and Speculative Masonry. They are taught that Operative Masonry relates to the practical craft of building, while Speculative Masonry applies these principles to moral and ethical self-improvement, emphasizing virtues like subduing passions, acting justly, maintaining good reputation, secrecy, and charity.

Candidates are taught the distinction between Operative and Speculative Masonry, with the latter emphasizing subduing passions, acting upon the square, maintaining a good report, secrecy, and charity. It highlights the obligation to pay rational homage to the Deity, viewing creation with reverence and inspiring exalted ideas of the Divine Creator. The contemplation of the globes encourages the studies of astronomy, geography, navigation, and related arts, which benefit society and improve the mind.

As Entered Apprentices in youth, we should diligently pursue useful knowledge. As Fellow Crafts in manhood, we must apply this knowledge to fulfill our duties to God, our neighbors, and ourselves. As Master Masons in old age, we should enjoy the positive reflections of a life well-spent and anticipate a glorious immortality.

Masons should strive for charity as extensive as the universality of Masonry. The three principal supports of Masonry (wisdom, strength, beauty) are fundamental to the craft. The three stages of human life (youth, manhood, age) remind Masons of their journey and development. The three degrees of Masonry represent a progressive path of knowledge. The five human senses, especially hearing, seeing, and feeling, are essential for Masonic recognition and communication, allowing Masons to know each other in both light and darkness. The seven liberal arts and sciences are crucial for intellectual development and understanding, emphasizing the importance of education within Masonry.

Candidates are taught to occupy their minds with useful knowledge in youth (as Entered Apprentices), apply that knowledge to duties to God, neighbors, and self in manhood (as Fellow Crafts), and in age (as Master Masons), enjoy the reflections of a well-spent life and hope for glorious immortality. They are also encouraged to cultivate a pure heart and constant gratitude to the Creator.

The candidate learns about the historical and structural grandeur of King Solomon's Temple, emphasizing the collaborative effort and diverse skills required for its construction. The pillars of Wisdom, Strength, and Beauty teach the necessity of these virtues in any great undertaking. The constitution and meeting places of the different lodges reinforce the hierarchical structure and progression within Masonry. The emblems provide moral and philosophical instruction, while the narrative of the lost word and its substitute underscores themes of loss, perseverance, and the eventual recovery of truth, encouraging continued pursuit of Masonic knowledge.
tradition_notes: Freemasonry is considered under two denominations: Operative and Speculative.
Ancient brethren wrought in both Operative and Speculative Masonry.
Modern Masons work in Speculative Masonry only.
Ancient brethren consecrated the seventh day as a day of rest, following God's creation pattern.
Josephus is cited as an informant regarding the temple's construction duration and the absence of rain.
Sacred history is referenced for the detail that no tool sounds were heard during the temple's building.
The placement of the two pillars is presented as a tradition to memorialize the deliverance from Egyptian bondage and the pillars of fire and cloud.
The practice of halving a 'secret' word was common in the 18th Century or earlier.
The status of the figure commemorated by the second pillar as Assistant High Priest is recorded in the Pseudepigrapha, not the Volume of the Sacred Law.
In the Grand Lodge of All England at York, it was customary up to 1780 to make men Apprentices and Fellows on the same evening.
A trace of this practice is still evident in the ritual through the Senior Warden's question.
Some Lodges, especially in large cities, employ real steps for the ascent, while most country Lodges use painted boards to represent the steps.

The height of the pillars is stated as thirty-five (35) cubits, but sources like Morgan, Richardson, Bernard, and Allyn state only eighteen (18) cubits. With chapiters of five cubits each, the total height is forty (40) cubits.
Ancient brethren worked six days and rested on the seventh, consecrating it as a day of rest, mirroring God's creation of the heavens and earth.
The pillars were cast hollow to better withstand inundation and conflagrations.
The hollow pillars are said to have contained the archives of Masonry.
The pillars Boaz and Jachin are named, and their individual and collective scriptural allusions are provided.
Hiram Abiff is identified as the Grand Master who cast the pillars and other holy vessels.
The specific location for casting the pillars is given as the banks of the river Jordan, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zaredatha.
The hollow nature of the pillars is explained as a means to withstand inundations or conflagrations and to contain the archives of Masonry.
The number seven has always been held in high estimation among Masons for various reasons, including its connection to the liberal arts and sciences.
The Master's carpet is described as a large map Lodges generally keep, embellished with Masonic emblems.
Josephus and sacred history are cited regarding the construction of King Solomon's Temple, noting no rain during the day and no sound of iron tools.
The constitution of a Lodge of Entered Apprentices is seven (one Master Mason, six Entered Apprentices), meeting on the Ground Floor of King Solomon's Temple.
The constitution of a Lodge of Fellow Crafts is five (two Master Masons, three Fellow Crafts), meeting in the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple.
The constitution of a Lodge of Master Masons is three (three Master Masons), meeting in the Sanctum Sanctorum or Holy of Holies of King Solomon's Temple.
The Master's carpet of emblems is explicitly stated to be a part of the initiation of the Third Degree.
It is a tradition that during the seven years of the Temple's construction, it did not rain in the daytime.
The word 'Mah-Hah-Bone' is presented as the substitute for the lost word in the Master Mason's Degree.
The original lost word was reportedly found after 470 years and is used in the Royal Arch Degree.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:deeper-meaning-of-fc-degree:section-0009 -->
### Enrichment from The Deeper Meaning of the FC Degree
Source section: The Secrets and the Ear of Corn

This section discusses the distinct nature of the secrets for each Masonic degree, contrasting the First Degree's focus on practical intelligence with the Second Degree's emphasis on the 'heart' or soul, representing intuition and affection. It introduces a Hebrew word signifying 'sprouting forth' as emblematic of the Fellow Craft candidate's spiritual growth. The ear of corn is presented as a central symbol for the candidate's soul growth and enlightenment, drawing parallels with ancient Egyptian and Eleusinian mysteries where corn symbolized immortality and revelation. The text also notes the use of corn in Masonic Lodge consecrations, tracing boards, and Grand Lodge officer regalia, emphasizing its personal symbolic application for meditation and transformation.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:deeper-meaning-of-fc-degree:section-0009 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0066 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section congratulates a Brother on being passed to the Second Degree, emphasizing that Freemasonry values internal qualifications over external ones. It highlights the institution's resilience, having survived the destruction of Solomon's magnificent Temple. The text explains that Masonic mysteries are transmitted through attentive listening and faithful preservation, using architectural tools and symbolic emblems to impart wise truths across generations. It concludes by reminding all Masons, from the youngest Entered Apprentice to the Worshipful Master, to humbly bow before the sacred name of Deity.

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The secrets of the First Degree relate to practical intelligence and active duties, while those of the Second Degree pertain to the 'heart' or soul, representing intuition and affection. The Hebrew word for this degree, signifying 'sprouting forth,' symbolizes the candidate's new life and spiritual germination. The ear of corn is a powerful symbol of the candidate's soul growth, nourished by the 'Living Water of Enlightenment,' indicating a spiritual transformation. In ancient rituals, corn symbolized eternity and immortality (Egyptian Rituals) and profound revelation (Eleusinian Mysteries). Within Masonry, the 'full corn in the ear' signifies the ripening of what was sown as bare grain into prolific fruit, representing the culmination of spiritual development.

The survival of Freemasonry despite the destruction of Solomon's Temple symbolizes the enduring nature and resilience of the institution, suggesting that its true essence is not in physical structures but in its principles and the hearts of its members. Architectural tools and symbolic emblems are used to represent and imprint wise and serious truths, serving as a visual and conceptual language for moral and philosophical instruction.

The Pot of Incense symbolizes a pure heart, which is considered an acceptable sacrifice to Deity. Its fervent glow represents the continuous gratitude our hearts should feel towards the great and beneficent Author of our existence for the blessings and comforts we enjoy.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate learns that the secrets of each degree are distinct, with the Second Degree focusing on the development of the 'heart' or soul. Through the symbolism of the 'sprouting forth' and the ear of corn, the candidate is taught that new life and spiritual growth have germinated within them as a result of their work and trials. They are encouraged to use the symbol of the ear of corn for personal meditation, allowing the idea to transform them until it ceases to be merely a symbol and becomes an internalized truth of their spiritual ripening.

The candidate is taught that true worth in Freemasonry comes from internal character and moral qualifications, not outward appearances or worldly status. They learn the importance of diligently receiving and faithfully preserving Masonic knowledge and mysteries. The lesson also instills a profound sense of reverence and humility towards the Deity, a principle that unites all Masons regardless of their degree or station.

Candidates should strive to cultivate a pure heart, which is an acceptable offering to the Divine, and maintain a constant sense of gratitude for the blessings bestowed upon them by the Creator.
tradition_notes: The Ritual states that 'The secrets of each Degree are to be kept separate and distinct from those in the former.'

In Egyptian Rituals, the Candidate declared 'I am a germ of eternity!' while holding an ear of corn fertilized by the sacred water of the Nile, and grains of corn were buried with the deceased as emblems of immortality.
In the Eleusinian Mysteries, an ear of corn was presented to the Candidate during one of the most advanced and secret initiation rites, revealing the 'mysteries of Ceres' associated with it.
The text references the historical and legendary destruction of Solomon's Temple, a significant event in Masonic tradition, and the subsequent survival of Freemasonry.

It describes the traditional method of transmitting Masonic mysteries verbally ('attentive ear receives the sound from the instructive tongue') and through memory ('safely lodged in the repository of faithful breasts').
The term 'carpet' in this context refers to a piece of cloth on which the emblems of the degrees are painted.

### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Entered Apprentice Degree: Historical Notes

This section provides historical notes and explanations for various elements of the Entered Apprentice Degree. It discusses the reverence associated with removing footwear in the East, linking it to the purity of the Mosaic pavement. It emphasizes that a candidate's admission is solely based on their avowal of belief and trust in God. Practical details are given regarding the number of candidates that can be initiated, passed, or raised in each degree. The text debunks claims that biblical figures like Adam, Moses, or Solomon were Freemasons. It clarifies the dual meaning of the word 'Hail' or 'Hale' in Masonic context. Specific ritual actions, such as drawing a hand or sword across the candidate's throat, are mentioned. The story of Hipparchus, a Pythagorean, is recounted as a cautionary tale about the severe consequences of violating societal engagements. The concept of 'Light' in initiation is explained as progressing from material to intellectual illumination. Finally, the symbolic meaning of the first sign, related to the palate and throat, is detailed as a lesson in temperance and restraint of passions, and the role of the Master and Senior Warden in laying the corner-stone is noted, along with the practice of fostering emulation and discussion in Lodge.

### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Fellow Craft Degree: Closing Notes

This section concludes the Fellow Craft Degree, offering detailed explanations and historical context through footnotes. It explores the historical assertion of St. John's connection to the Essenes as a secret society conveying moral truths through symbols. The text describes a ritualistic gesture involving a rake across the breast, emphasizing the penalty of the obligation and symbolizing fortitude against adversity and the importance of fidelity to secrets. It introduces the Jewish Cabala's rule of Transposition, applying it to Hebrew words like ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) to signify moral and physical light. A comprehensive explanation of 'Shibboleth' is provided, detailing its dual meanings (ear of grain, stream of water) and its historical use by the Gileadites to identify Ephraimites based on speech peculiarities. Finally, it outlines the Fellow Craft Lecture, covering the passing ceremony, the distinction between operative and speculative Masonry, the study of liberal arts and sciences, and the legend of wages paid in the Middle Chamber, guarded by the Junior and Senior Wardens.

### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Most Excellent Master: Ceremony of Completion

This section details the 'Ceremony of Completion' for the Most Excellent Master degree, celebrating the symbolic completion of the Temple. It includes the singing of an 'Excellent Master's Song' which marks the end of labor and the laying of the cope-stone and keystone. The ritual involves placing the keystone into an arch supported by pillars Jachin and Boaz, secured with six gavel raps. Brethren divest themselves of working tools and regalia, hanging them on the arch, signifying the completion of their work. The Ark is brought forward and placed on the altar with a pot of incense. The ceremony culminates in the acceptance and investiture of worthy candidates as Most Excellent Masters, a sign of admiration or astonishment, a prayer for divine presence, and a reading from 2 Chronicles vii. 1, 4, followed by a ritual involving fire and repeated declarations of God's mercy. The Master then delivers a charge to the candidate, emphasizing the duty to dispense light and truth and the importance of acquiring knowledge.

This section details the completion and closing ceremonies of the Most Excellent Master degree, including specific ritualistic actions, a charge to the newly admitted candidate, and readings from Psalms. The charge emphasizes the candidate's duty to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons and to diligently acquire knowledge. The closing ritual involves brethren assembling around the altar, kneeling, joining hands, and performing balancing movements, followed by a lecture explaining the significance of the cap stone and the six distinct knocks for admission.

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The ceremony symbolizes an upward step in the candidate's evolution, an exploration of a higher level of being, and a transition from the physical to the spiritual realm through an intermediate region of mind or soul. It represents the striving for intellectual light and mental illumination.

The survival of Freemasonry despite the destruction of Solomon's Temple symbolizes the enduring nature and resilience of the institution, suggesting that its true essence is not in physical structures but in its principles and the hearts of its members. Architectural tools and symbolic emblems are used to represent and imprint wise and serious truths, serving as a visual and conceptual language for moral and philosophical instruction.

Freemasonry primarily regards the internal, not the external, qualifications of a man. Geometry is established as the basis of the Masonic art, possessing a divine and moral nature. It is seen as a science that not only proves the wonderful properties of nature but also demonstrates the more important truths of morality.

The proclamation symbolizes the public declaration of Masonic principles, including loyalty to country, reverence for the Great Architect, and the practice of benevolence. The handing over of the working tools (Square, Level, Plumb) to the Architect by the Grand Master symbolizes the trust placed in skilled leadership and the importance of proper design, execution, and moral rectitude in all undertakings, particularly in the construction of a symbolic or literal temple. It signifies the transition from planning to execution, guided by Masonic virtues.

The proclamation emphasizes that Masonic secrets are lawful, honorable, and inviolable, entrusted to Masons. The longevity and esteemed membership of the Fraternity symbolize its inherent goodness and honor. The verification of the foundation stone as 'square, level and plumb, true and trusty' symbolizes the integrity, precision, and adherence to foundational principles within Masonry, both in physical construction and moral conduct. God is referred to as the 'Great Architect of all things'.

Divine aid symbolizes the necessity of God's help for spiritual advancement and the attainment of Masonic privileges. The Square symbolizes the need for skill (manual and mental) and the moral imperative to act with integrity and fairness ('on the square') towards all people.

The specific attire and the prominent placement of the Square during the obligation emphasize the practical and moral principles associated with the Fellow Craft degree. The Square, as a working tool, symbolizes rectitude, morality, and the proper conduct of life, reinforced by its presence in the degree's signs and the candidate's posture.

The Mosaic pavement is accounted pure and immaculate, symbolizing that no pollution can be tolerated on that sacred floor. The word 'Hail' (from Saxon 'Hael') is used as a salutation, inquiring 'Whence do you hail?' The word 'Hale' (from Saxon 'helan') signifies to conceal, referring to the 'tye.' Light is the first demand of a candidate at initiation, with material light succeeded by intellectual illumination. The palate and throat are considered the chief seat of irregular appetites, and the first sign instructs Masons to avoid temptation through proper restraint on passions, promoting temperance, decency, and decorum.

The duegard of a Fellow Craft alludes to the manner in which the candidate's hands were placed when taking the solemn oath of a Fellow Craft. The sign of a Fellow Craft alludes to the penalty of the obligation.

Secret societies like the Essenes conveyed moral truths under symbolical figures. The breast, as the abode of fortitude, teaches the candidate to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity with patience rather than betraying secrets. Hebrew words ZAOB (fortify) and NIKAJ (prepared) are symbolically interpreted to mean fortifying oneself with higher sciences and preparing to receive moral light. Shibboleth signifies both an ear of grain and a stream of water, with its symbolic interpretation increasing interest as one advances through the 'field of corn by the river-side'. The payment of wages without fear or scruple symbolizes the employers' entitlement to unlimited confidence.

The completion of the Temple signifies the culmination of labor and the achievement of a significant spiritual or architectural goal. The cope-stone and keystone represent the final, essential elements that bind the structure together, symbolizing perfection and stability. The divestment of working tools indicates that the physical labor is complete, and the focus shifts to the spiritual and intellectual. The Ark being safely seated on the altar with incense symbolizes the divine presence and acceptance of the completed work. The sign of admiration and the prayer for divine glory emphasize the sacredness of the institution and the aspiration for divine guidance and inspiration. The fire from heaven and the glory of the Lord filling the house signify divine approval and the sacredness of the consecrated space.

The cap stone symbolizes the completion of King Solomon's Temple, upon the dedication ceremonies of which this degree is founded. The six distinct knocks allude to the Sixth Degree of Masonry.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The candidate is expected to attain intellectual light and mental illumination, understand and control a higher level of their own being (mind/soul), and pass through a transitional phase of personal experience towards spiritual development and mastership.

The candidate is taught that true worth in Freemasonry comes from internal character and moral qualifications, not outward appearances or worldly status. They learn the importance of diligently receiving and faithfully preserving Masonic knowledge and mysteries. The lesson also instills a profound sense of reverence and humility towards the Deity, a principle that unites all Masons regardless of their degree or station.

The Fellow Craft candidate is taught that true worth lies in internal character and that increasing knowledge leads to improved social interaction. They are reminded of their duties to uphold Masonic laws and to interact with brethren with fairness, kindness, and justice. A key lesson is the importance of intellectual and moral self-improvement through the study of the liberal arts, with a particular focus on geometry as a foundation for both natural and moral understanding. The candidate is expected to maintain virtuous conduct, consistent with the principles of the Institution.

The candidate learns about the public-facing virtues of Freemasonry, including loyalty, piety, and universal benevolence. They also learn the importance of skill, capacity, and diligent conduct in leadership roles, as exemplified by the Architect entrusted with the building project. The emphasis on order, harmony, beauty, and strength in construction serves as an allegory for the moral and spiritual building of character.

Candidates learn that Freemasonry is a lawful and honorable institution, whose members are devoted to God and country, and committed to universal benevolence. They are taught that the Craft possesses ancient, unrevealed secrets that are held in trust. The ceremony of laying a foundation stone demonstrates the importance of precision, integrity, and adherence to ancient customs in all Masonic endeavors.

A candidate's progress should be marked by genuine understanding of the Craft, not just memorization. Advancement should not be automatic but based on demonstrated learning and readiness.

Candidates are taught that their own efforts are insufficient for true progress in the Second Degree and that divine assistance is essential. They are also reminded of the importance of developing both practical and intellectual skills, and of conducting themselves with honesty and integrity in all interactions.

The preparation, including the specific dress and interaction with the Square, teaches the candidate about the historical roots of Masonic practice and the enduring importance of the Square as a symbol of moral conduct and the duties expected in the Fellow Craft degree.

Admission into the fraternity is based on a sincere belief and trust in God, and where God's name is invoked, no danger can ensue. It is unwise to assert more than can be proven or argue against probability regarding historical claims. The story of Hipparchus serves as a severe caution against violating engagements, emphasizing the importance of truth and fidelity, and the dire consequences of their breach. Candidates are taught that the pursuit of 'Light' extends beyond the material to intellectual understanding. The first sign teaches the importance of temperance in all indulgences, self-restraint, and never exceeding the boundary of decency and decorum, under penalty of violating Masonic engagements.

The candidate learns the specific grips, words, and signs of the Fellow Craft degree, including the pass grip, real grip, the word 'Jachin', the duegard, the sign, and the pass word 'Shibboleth'. They are instructed on the proper way to wear the Fellow Craft apron, with the flap down and a corner turned up, symbolizing the practice during the building of King Solomon's Temple. The candidate is also introduced to the working tools of a Fellow Craft: the plumb, square, and level.

Candidates are taught to suppress apprehension and discontent, enduring adversity, distress, pain, or disappointment with patience. This fortitude prevents the bitter stings of remorse that result from betraying secrets entrusted under solemn obligation. The neophyte should prepare to receive moral light through higher sciences. Candidates are directed to an attentive study of the liberal arts and sciences. The legend of wages paid without fear or scruple teaches the importance of unlimited confidence in employers.

The candidate's admission to the Most Excellent Master degree is a recognition of their Masonic abilities and an encouragement to maintain that esteem through good conduct and adherence to rules. A primary duty of a Most Excellent Master is to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons, which necessitates an accurate and comprehensive acquaintance with the lectures of all degrees. The candidate is urged to apply double diligence to acquire knowledge and information to properly discharge their duties and preserve the unsullied title of Most Excellent Master.

The candidate's admission is a testament to the brethren's good opinion of their Masonic abilities, which should not be forfeited by misconduct. As a Most Excellent Master, the candidate has a great duty to dispense light and truth to uninformed Masons, requiring an accurate acquaintance with the lectures of each degree. Diligence in acquiring knowledge is essential to properly discharge duties and preserve the title of Most Excellent Master.

entries.title: Perambulation: The Soul's Forward Movement
entries.short_summary: In the Fellow Craft degree, the perambulations around the Lodge symbolize the human soul's continuous search for enlightenment and spiritual growth, representing a forward movement towards a spiritual goal. The Deacon guiding the candidate symbolizes an unerring enlightened conscience or inner guide. Salutes to the Master signify recognition of higher spiritual powers within oneself, and examinations by the Wardens (representing the psyche and intellect) symbolize the testing of one's qualifications for spiritual advancement.
entries.full_summary:
In the Fellow Craft degree, the perambulations around the Lodge symbolize the human soul's continuous search for enlightenment and spiritual growth, representing a forward movement towards a spiritual goal. The Deacon guiding the candidate symbolizes an unerring enlightened conscience or inner guide. Salutes to the Master signify recognition of higher spiritual powers within oneself, and examinations by the Wardens (representing the psyche and intellect) symbolize the testing of one's qualifications for spiritual advancement.

### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Divine Aid and the Square at the Entrance

To attain the privileges of the Second Degree, divine aid is necessary, as human effort alone is insufficient. This spiritual lesson is a pervasive theme in English Masonry. The Square, presented at the entrance, symbolizes the need for both manual and mental skill, expected of a fully trained apprentice, and serves as a reminder to act 'on the square' with all humanity.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0003 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0005 -->
### Enrichment from Commentary on the Second Degree
Source section: Passing the Junior Warden: The Gate of the Lodge

This section details the historical and symbolic significance of passing the Junior Warden in the Fellow Craft degree. It explains that the practice originates from 17th-century Guild Masonry, where Masons were questioned for passwords before a lodge could open in a new degree. In early Moderns lodges, Wardens guarded the 'gate' of the lodge, each associated with a pillar. The Junior Warden's word of passing grants the candidate permission to enter his part of the lodge gate, with full access to a Lodge of Fellows granted after passing the second pillar.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:commentary-on-the-second-degree:section-0005 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0045 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Section First.

This section details the ritualistic Q&A for the Fellow Craft degree, covering the candidate's preparation, admission process, and the initial teachings received. It describes being tried by the square, divesting of metals, the significance of the cable-tow, the three knocks and their allusion to the three jewels of a Fellow Craft, and the reception on the angle of the square. The perambulation, the specific steps to the East, the due form of taking the obligation on the Holy Bible, square, and compasses, and the discovery of one point of the compasses elevated are explained. It also covers the presentation of the pass, token, grip, and word, the proper wearing of the Fellow Craft apron, and the presentation and symbolic use of the working tools: the plumb, square, and level. The section concludes with the instruction regarding the ascent by winding stairs to the Middle Chamber of King Solomon's Temple for further lessons on wages and jewels.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0045 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0046 -->
### Enrichment from Duncan's Masonic Ritual and Monitor of Freemasonry
Source section: Second Section.

This section, presented as a Q&A, explores the historical work of Fellow Crafts in both speculative and operative Masonry, specifically at the building of King Solomon's Temple. It details the tradition of working six days and resting on the seventh, explaining this practice as a commemoration of God's creation of the heavens and earth, allowing for contemplation of divine works and adoration of the Creator. The dialogue also briefly mentions a return to the sanctum sanctorum of King Solomon's Temple.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:duncans-ritual-monitor-1866:section-0046 -->
